prompt_text: Alternatives to Vellum for AI engineering teams building and deploying LLM-powered products
response:
For AI engineering teams, the main **Vellum alternatives** fall into four buckets: *LLM ops platforms*, *visual/low‑code workflow builders*, *frameworks/libraries*, and *multi‑vendor orchestration APIs*. Your best choice depends on whether you care most about observability & evals, visual workflow building, or flexibility for custom infra.

Below is a concise map of credible alternatives, focused on teams building and deploying production LLM features.

---

### 1. “Vellum-like” LLM ops & workflow platforms

These are closest to Vellum’s positioning: prompt/version management, evaluation, tracing, and deployment for LLM workflows.

| Tool | What it’s good for vs Vellum |
| --- | --- |
| **Sandgarden** | Production-focused runtime that “automatically crafts the pipeline of tools and processes needed to experiment with AI” and move from test to production, with built‑in deploy/monitor/scale capabilities.[2] Strong fit if you want an **enterprise AI runtime** and fast path from PoC to production with less infra work. |
| **Stack AI** | Low‑code UI to build **AI agents** and workflows via drag‑and‑drop, with many prebuilt templates (chatbots, back office automation, basic RAG, etc.).[2] Better if you want **non‑technical users** to configure flows while engineering focuses on integrations and governance. |
| **Runbear** (from SourceForge alt listing) | No‑code platform for **AI agents integrated with Slack, Teams, HubSpot, etc.**[7] Good when the core need is agentic workflows embedded in existing tools rather than deep custom infra. |

These are good options if your priority is *productionization, collaboration, and governance* over raw framework flexibility.

---

### 2. Visual / low‑code workflow builders (more generic than Vellum)

These help teams design complex LLM workflows visually, often mixing LLM calls, tools, and APIs.

| Tool | Key points |
| --- | --- |
| **Eden AI** | Multi‑provider orchestration via a **single API** across OpenAI, Google, AWS, etc., plus a **no‑code/low‑code workflow builder**.[1] Strong if you want **provider abstraction + cost optimization** and simple, drag‑and‑drop automated flows (translate → summarize → sentiment, routing, etc.).[1] |
| **Flowise AI** | Open‑source **low‑code, node‑based editor** to visually wire LLMs, APIs, vector DBs, and tools.[1] Good for teams that want self‑hosting and a visual interface similar to Vellum’s canvas but are comfortable managing their own infra. |
| **Stack AI** (again) | Similar category, but more opinionated around **agents** and business workflows with templates.[2] |

These are particularly attractive if product and data teams want to contribute without writing much code, and engineering is okay standardizing on the platform’s runtime.

---

### 3. Developer‑first frameworks & libraries (build-your-own-Vellum stack)

If your team is comfortable with Python/TypeScript and wants maximum control, these frameworks provide the primitives that Vellum productizes (RAG, chains, agents, etc.) but *without* managed evaluations, dashboards, and governance out of the box.

| Framework | How it compares |
| --- | --- |
| **LangChain** | General‑purpose framework for orchestrating **chains, tools, RAG pipelines, agents, memory, and prompt management**.[1] Highly flexible but requires engineering ownership of infra, testing, and observability. Great if you want to *embed* LLM logic directly in your codebase and build your own evaluation and monitoring layer. |
| **Haystack** | Open‑source framework focused on **search and RAG pipelines**: document stores (Elasticsearch, FAISS, Pinecone), retrievers, readers, etc.[1] Ideal if your main AI features are **retrieval-heavy** (QA over docs, semantic search) and you’re okay with more coding and DevOps work. |
| **LlamaIndex** | Data-centric framework for **ingesting, indexing, and retrieving structured & unstructured data** into LLM workflows, with integrations to many vector DBs.[1] Excellent for **knowledge retrieval, data-heavy assistants, and complex indexing strategies**, with you owning deployment and monitoring. |

These tools are powerful “building blocks” for a custom LLM platform; many teams pair them with in‑house dashboards or generic observability (e.g., logging, tracing) to approximate Vellum’s eval/tracing story.

---

### 4. Multi‑provider orchestration & automation platforms

Broader AI automation platforms that can cover part of Vellum’s use case, especially for integrating LLM workflows into existing tools and business processes.
